mnemonics.title: NPV Decision Rule
mnemonics.content: Positive NPV = Go! Negative NPV = No Go!

mnemonics.title: Payback Period Quick Formula
mnemonics.content: PBP = Initial Investment ÷ Annual Cash Inflow

mnemonics.title: CBA Steps
mnemonics.content: CBA = Identify Costs + Benefits → Quantify → Discount → Compare → Decide

acronyms.usage: Systematic evaluation of project economics comparing costs and benefits in monetary terms
Difference between present value of cash inflows and outflows; positive NPV indicates viability
Discount rate that makes NPV zero; higher IRR = more attractive project
Percentage return calculated as (Net Profit / Cost) × 100
Ratio of benefits to costs; BCR > 1 means benefits exceed costs
Time required to recover initial investment from net cash inflows
Net operating profit after tax minus cost of capital invested

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a seesaw with benefits on one side and costs on the other. Discounting is like adding weights to the cost side to account for time value. If benefits side goes down, NPV is negative.
Think of a horizontal line (total costs) and upward-sloping line (total benefits). The intersection point is break-even - below it is loss zone, above it is profit zone.
Use a funnel analogy: higher discount rate = narrower funnel = smaller present value. Lower discount rate = wider funnel = larger present value.
Picture a bucket being filled with water (cash inflows). The time to fill to the bucket's rim (initial investment) is the payback period.

analogies.analogy: NPV is like comparing the value of money to a tree. A sapling (future money) is worth less than a full-grown tree (present money) of the same species.
Discounting is like aging wine. A bottle of wine (future cash) is worth less than the same bottle aged today (present cash) because you could sell it now and invest the proceeds.
Intangible benefits are like the reputation of a restaurant - you can't put a precise rupee value on it, but it definitely adds value to the business.
Sensitivity analysis is like stress-testing a bridge by applying different weights and seeing how it affects the structure's stability.
Opportunity cost is like choosing a movie to watch - if you pick Action movie, the romantic comedy you didn't pick is your opportunity cost.
